coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-magnetic nanocomposites for the detection and removal of Hg2+
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Ruyan
Qu, Yaoyao
Tang, Mingyu
Zhao, Jingqiang
Chua, Sophie
Li, Tingting
Zhang, Fang
E. H. Wheatley, Andrew
Chai, Fang
coredata.dc:description: The dual functional detection and removal of heavy ion metals by carbon dots has become an urgent matter of concern. Here, a unique fluorescent carbon dot-magnetic nanocomposite (Fe3O4/CDs) was prepared by hydrothermal methods for sensitive detection of Hg2+. The Fe3O4/CDs serve as fluorescent probes with higher selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+, with the lowest detectable limit of 0.3 nM. Hg2+ statically quenched the blue emission of Fe3O4/CDs, which can be restored in the presence of saturated EDTA solution. The utilization of Fe3O4/CDs was fulfilled by recovering their emission conveniently. The recovery of Hg2+ in Chagan Lake water, tap water and drinks was calculated at 96.5 ~ 108.8%, which demonstrates the feasibility of the Fe3O4/CDs sensing system in natural samples. Notably, the Fe3O4/CDs can drive the effective removal of Hg2+ from samples, which is of outstanding significance as a promising probe in environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fe3O4
Heavy metal ion
Fluorescence
Detection
Removal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing and removal of Hg2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. TEM image (scale bar is 20 nm) and B. high resolution TEM image of Fe3O4/CDs (scale bar is 5 nm), C. XRD of Fe3O4 and Fe3O4/CDs compared with JCPDS No. 77–1545.

(A) Absorption, excitation and emission of the Fe3O4/CDs (inset: photo images under daylight and UV light). (B) emission of Fe3O4/CDs under excitation of 280–420 nm. (C) Fluorescence decay curve of the Fe3O4/CDs.
Magnetization hysteresis loops of Fe3O4/CDs measured at room temperature, coercive field from the hysteresis loop (inset-up), and dispersions under magnetic field for 30 s (inset-down).
FL emission of probe with common metal ions (100 μM). Inset: corresponding images under 365 nm UV lamp.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Fe3O4/CDs exposing diverse concentrations of Hg2+ (0–300 μM). (B) The linear plots of I/I0 versus the Hg2+ concentrations (0.001–0.01 μM, R2 = 0.997). (C) The corresponding images under UV lamp.
Strategy of synthesis Fe3O4/CDs and utility in sensing and removal of Hg2+.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-magnetic nanocomposites for the detection and removal of Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Cambridge, Lensfield Rd, Cambridge CB2 1EW, UK
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Cambridge, Lensfield Rd, Cambridge CB2 1EW, UK

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The dual functional detection and removal of heavy ion metals by carbon dots has become an urgent matter of concern. Here, a unique fluorescent carbon dot-magnetic nanocomposite (Fe3O4/CDs) was prepared by hydrothermal methods for sensitive detection of Hg2+. The Fe3O4/CDs serve as fluorescent probes with higher selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+, with the lowest detectable limit of 0.3 nM. Hg2+ statically quenched the blue emission of Fe3O4/CDs, which can be restored in the presence of saturated EDTA solution. The utilization of Fe3O4/CDs was fulfilled by recovering their emission conveniently. The recovery of Hg2+ in Chagan Lake water, tap water and drinks was calculated at 96.5 ~ 108.8%, which demonstrates the feasibility of the Fe3O4/CDs sensing system in natural samples. Notably, the Fe3O4/CDs can drive the effective removal of Hg2+ from samples, which is of outstanding significance as a promising probe in environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions, even at trace level, are considered extremely dangerous to life. In contemporary society, with the development of industry and the improper discharge of industrial wastewater, the pollution of heavy metal ions has become a major point of concern (Sahoo et al., 2019). As one of the most noxious heavy metal ions, Hg2+ exhibits damaging effects on all creatures in the world because of possible food chain transmission (He et al., 2020). Hg2+ can also damage the respiratory and digestive tracts just through absorption by the skin (Babaee, Barati, Gholivand, Taherpour, Zolfaghar, & Shamsipur, 2019). The Environmental Protection Agency (USA) stipulates that the concentration of Hg2+ in water should not exceed 10 nM (Yu, Qu, Chai, & Chen, 2018). The presence of Hg2+ in environmental water such as surface water, underground water can be hugely crucial, causing public hazards and affecting the safety of the food-chain (Su et al., 2014). To avoid the harm of Hg2+ in contaminated water, the concentration of Hg2+ in the environment, water and drinks should be monitored and assessed simultaneously.

Therefore, there is an urgent need for a simple and effective method for detecting Hg2+ in food, drinks, surface water, underground water and contaminated water, because water plays an indispensable and ubiquitous role in food safety and daily production and life. Several analytical methods have been established to detect Hg2+ ions so far, such as atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy (Bagheri & Naderi, 2009), ion chromatography (Zhou, Xing, & Zhao, 2014), ICP-MS (Leopold, Foulkes, & Worsfold, 2010), electrochemical methods (Manna & Raj, 2018) and X-ray absorption spectroscopy (Tholkappian et al., 2018). These methods have achieved great success, but also exhibited limitations such as the need for instrumental facilities, and complicated processes of pretreatment and operation, which restrict their potential for on-site application. However, detection in situ is necessary for the monitoring of waters. Thus, a portable, sensitive and quick response probe for heavy metal ions is of urgent need. Out of the various types of sensors, fluorescent probes are widely used due to their simple operation and rapid response, which is expected to be exploited within an on-site probe. Additionally, for the need of sustainable development, a recyclable probe with removal properties is highly desired.

In recent years, environmental analysis techniques such as nanomaterial-based fluorescent sensors, for example gold nanoclusters (NCs) (Bian et al., 2017), silicon nanoparticles (Tang et al., 2020), and carbon dots(CDs) (Zhou, Lin, Huang, Ren, & Qu, 2012) have been widely developed. Among them, fluorescent CDs have attracted much attention for their outstanding optical properties, good biocompatibility, low cost and non-toxicity (Li, Wang, Zhang, Chai, & Huang, 2018; Wang, et al., 2017). CDs have therefore been popularly used in medical diagnosis (Zhong, Yang, Wang, & Yang, 2017), electrocatalysis (Dhenadhayalan, Lin, & Saleh, 2020), bioimaging (Bhamore, Jha, Park, & Kailasa, 2018), drug delivery (Medeiros, Manioudakis, Noun, Macairan, Victoria, & Naccache, 2019) and tracing heavy metal ions (Zhang, Wang, Xiao, Wang, Wang, & Feng, 2017), and molecules like nitro compounds, hydrogen peroxide, picric acid etc. (Campos et al., 2016; Costas-Mora, Romero, Lavilla, & Bendicho, 2015; Fan et al., 2017). In recent years, research focusing on the detection of heavy metal ions in aqueous solutions has received extensive attention. Since Qu’s group reported the first fluorescent CDs probe for detecting Hg2+, a new avenue of CDs based fluorescent sensors with promising advantages such as being sensitive, simple, and low cost has been revealed (Qu, Wang, Ren, & Qu, 2013; Zhou, Lin, Huang, Ren, & Qu, 2012). Jiang et al reported a series of CDs based fluorescent probes for sensing Hg2+ ions (Zhou, et al., 2016). Notably, they explored a dual-emission CDs based colorimetric paper sensor which can analyze and readout Pb2+ assisted by an RGB recognizing App on a cell phone (Wang, et al., 2019). CDs have been extended, reformed and widely applied with their multiple merits. Our group has designed a dual-functional ratiometric fluorescent probe based on BSA-Au NCs and CDs with different ratios, which can recognize Hg2+ and Cr6+ simultaneously by different fluorescent colors (Yu, Zhang, Ren, Li, Zhu, Chai, et al., 2018). The dual emissive probe exhibits the combined pink fluorescent color, which responds to Cr6+ by quenching the blue emission of CDs, and recognizes Hg2+ with the shutting down of the red fluorescence of BSA-Au NCs. It therefore fulfills colorimetric and fluorimetric recognition of Hg2+ and Cr6+ separately and simultaneously. Although CDs can be used as fluorescent sensors for monitoring heavy metal ions, further research and extension is still expected to exploit them further. Endowing them with magnetic properties has permitted CDs to provide magnetic collection behavior, indicating that CDs can fulfill the dual the function of detection and recovery of Hg2+ (Tang, Ren, & Chai, 2020). The process of detection is crucial to protecting the environment, and as such the removal and distribution of the heavy metal ions has acquired increasing concern. The eventual purpose of detection and monitoring heavy metal ions is the removal of the heavy metal ions from the environment. Thus, how to collect unsuitable Hg2+ from water resources effectively is an extreme challenge which requires great effort. The removal of heavy metal ions from water sources has become an essential part of solving environmental problems.

Recently, some efficient technologies have been proposed for removing heavy metals from water, such as chemical precipitation, ion exchange, adsorption, electrochemical technology, and membrane filtration (Fu & Wang, 2011). Among them, the adsorption process has become the most prominent technique for realizing heavy metal removal from wastewater owing to its reversible nature and suitable desorption processes for multiple uses (Hua, Zhang, Pan, Zhang, Lv, & Zhang, 2012). The adsorption process, especially based on nano metal oxide, including Fe3O4/Fe2O3, MnO2, Al2O3, TiO2, MgO and CeO2, has shown great potential for heavy metal removal from water systems. The ease of synthesis and availability of resources render Fe2O3/Fe3O4 to be outstanding adsorbents for heavy metal sorption (Liu, Zhao, & Jiang, 2008; Wang, Zheng, Shao, Liu, Xu, & Zhu, 2010). Recently, Fe3O4/CDs hybrid nanoparticles have been prepared for detecting glutathione by colorimetric signal (Luo et al., 2019), which provided a valuablereferenceandideasfor dual-functional sensor design. CDs can be used directly to detect heavy metal ions, and both Fe3O4 and CDs are environmentally friendly. Thus, it is beneficial to integrate detection and recycling to explore magnetic functional CDs for protecting water resources with negligible risks of secondary contamination.

Herein, we develop a novel dual-purpose Fe3O4-carbon dots nanocomposite (Fe3O4/CDs) based magnetic fluorescent probe for the detection and recovery of Hg (II) using a general hydrothermal method. The as-prepared Fe3O4/CDs can be utilized as an effective fluorescent probe for monitoring Hg2+ through a turn off strategy. The Fe3O4/CDs have the advantages of long fluorescence lifetime, good water solubility and stability. The Fe3O4/CDs can be recycled by adding an EDTA solution. Notably, the magnetic properties of Fe3O4/CDs can also remove Hg2+ from the polluted water, which endows Fe3O4/CDs a promising advantage of collecting Hg2+ from the water conveniently. Furthermore, detecting Hg2+ in lake water was attempted and satisfactory results were obtained.

All chemical reagents used were of analytical grade. Diethylenetriamine (DETA) was obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai), Melamine and ammonium ferric citrate were provided by the Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Edetate disodium (EDTA·2Na) and other chemicals were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Company (Tianjin). All the experimental water was double distilled water.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were characterized by a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 TEM at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The magnetic behavior was investigated by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) Lake Shore 7410 system at room temperature. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected by a JASCOFT/IR-420. The UV–vis spectra were recorded on UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were acquired by Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA) and a PerkinElmer LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer. And fluorescence lifetime was recorded by a full-function fluorescence spectrometer (FLS920).

Firstly, melamine (2 g) and ammonium ferric citrate (0.5 g) were dissolved in a mixed solvent with H2O and DETA (volume ratio 9:1, 10 mL) as a precursor solution. Successively, the preceding solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave heated at 200 °C for 6 h. After natural cooling to ambient temperature, a brown Fe3O4/CDs suspension was obtained. The prepared Fe3O4/CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further use. In order to investigate the optimal condition of synthesis, the reaction at different temperature and with reaction time was also discussed in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing and removal of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: All experiments were performed at room temperature. The quenching effect of Hg2+ on fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4/CDs was conducted as follows. To elucidate the sensing behavior of Fe3O4/CDs, the solution was diluted 5000 times. Then the detection behavior of Fe3O4/CDs as probe was evaluated. The volume ratio of probe with each sample was 1:1, and fluorescence spectra of all samples were collected by a fluorescence spectrometer with excitation at 360 nm. For sensing Hg2+, the probe Fe3O4/CDs were exposed to a variety of concentrations of Hg2+ (0–300 μM), and then their fluorescence intensity was measured separately. The specificity was evaluated by introducing analogical metal ions (Zn2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe3+and As3+,100 μM) instead of Hg2+ (100 μM). For removal, the as-prepared Fe3O4/CDs were first mixed with a certain concentration of Hg2+ solution. After sufficient dispersion, the particles were immediately separated using a magnet, and a clear solution was obtained. After separation from the probe, the concentration of Hg2+ left in solution was analyzed by ICP-AES. The actual lake water was derived from Chagan Lake, tap water was taken from the lab, and drinks were purchased from the campus supermarket. All samples were treated by first filtering with a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, and then successively spiking with various concentrations of Hg2+.

At room temperature, a saturated solution of EDTA can restore the fluorescent emission of the used probe solution. The saturated EDTA solution was dropped into a detected sample to restore the fluorescence of probe. The detailed procedure for recovering fluorescence of Fe3O4/CDs was as follows. First, a concentration of 100 μM of Hg2+ was detected by the probe to form a reacted fluorescent sensing platform, designated Fe3O4/CDs-Hg2+. Then, different concentrations of EDTA were exposed to Fe3O4/CDs-Hg2+ to capture the Hg2+ and restore the fluorescence of the probe.

The Fe3O4/CDs were synthesized and employed as a recyclable fluorescent sensor to monitor and remove heavy metal ion Hg2+. The process of synthesis and application is illustrated in the Scheme 1 . The melamine, and ammonium ferric citrate were used as carbon and iron resource respectively. The final result was a brown suspension containing Fe3O4/CDs, and the diluted solution exhibited a bright blue fluorescent emission (λ = 365 nm) under ultraviolet light. The synthesis of Fe3O4/CDs at different temperature and reaction time was firstly studied, and the optimal reaction temperature and time of synthesizing Fe3O4/CDs was 200 ℃ (Fig. S1) and 6 h (Fig. S2), respectively. The emission exhibited a dependable “turn-off” response to Hg2+, which endowed the Fe3O4/CDs as a promising reliable fluorescent probe. Furthermore, the EDTA solution can make Fe3O4/CDs recover their fluorescence after suffering from quenching by Hg2+, which suggested that the Fe3O4/CDs can be utilized as a recyclable sensor. Notably, the Fe3O4/CDs successfully collected the Hg2+ in water using a permanent magnet, which confirmed the dual functionality of Fe3O4/CDs.

The morphology of the Fe3O4/CDs was first studied by TEM. Representative TEM images revealed Fe3O4/CDs composites of approximately spherical shape but with an undesirably broad range of sizes with mean diameter of 7.73 nm (Fig. 1 A). The HRTEM image showed high crystallinity of the Fe3O4/CDs in Fig. 1B. As demonstrated, the lattice spacings of 0.25 nm and 0.48 nm were assigned to the (311) and (111) lattice planes of Fe3O4 respectively (Medeiros, Manioudakis, Noun, Macairan, Victoria, & Naccache, 2019), and the spacings of 0.29 nm and 0.30 nm could be attributed to the (002) crystal plane of graphene (inset of Fig. 1A) (Wang, Wu, Fang, Guo, & Wang, 2019). Further survey of Fig. 1B proved that the Fe3O4 nanoparticles tended to be concentrated together and CDs distributed in the outer layer, which benefitted giving enough scope to express detecting behavior. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) was carried out to evaluate the solubility and dispersivity of the Fe3O4/CDs. As portrayed in Fig. S3, the particles in the aqueous solution rendered a uniform size distribution and good dispersion in DLS, which provided further validation of the favorable water solubility of the Fe3O4/CDs. The sizedistributionsare much broader forDLS measurements than TEM, which may be related to a swelling process (Nezhad-Mokhtari, Arsalani, Ghorbani, & Hamishehkar, 2018) in aqueous solution and small amount of aggregation. Considering the sizedistributions from DLS can often be almost 2x higher than TEM (Souza, Ciminelli, & Mohallem, 2016), the little difference in this work indicated the good dispersity of Fe3O4/CDs.

The XRD analysis was necessary to investigate the contents of the Fe3O4/CDs. To compare their composition intuitively, Fe3O4 NPs was synthesized by the same method using ammonium ferric citrate only. As proved in Fig. 1C, both the Fe3O4/CDs and Fe3O4 NPs exhibited accordant reflections of (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440), which was exactly the same as the standard card of Fe3O4 (JCPDS card No. 77–1545). The characteristic diffraction of CDs which emerged at 20-30˚ (Medeiros, Manioudakis, Noun, Macairan, Victoria, & Naccache, 2019) was not observed, though this was not taken as evidence that no CDs existed. Compared with excellent crystallinity of Fe3O4, although no clear diffraction appeared, the pattern of Fe3O4/CDs took on rough noise especially at 20-30˚, which might be representative of the amorphous nature and poor crystallinity of the carbon (Wang, Wu, Fang, Guo, & Wang, 2019).

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. S4) provided chemical bonding and molecular structures of the Fe3O4/CDs. The absorption band at a wavelength of 1105 cm−1 may be caused by the stretching vibration of CN. The typical peak at 1670 cm−1 was ascribed to a CO stretching vibration. The stretching vibration of –CH2 was located at 2338 cm−1 and bending and stretching vibrations of OH was observed at around 3340 cm−1. The band located at 1502 cm−1 belonged to the –NH2 stretching vibration (Yang, Tammina, Li, & Yang, 2019), which indicated the presence of an amino group-containing functional group on the surface of the Fe3O4/CDs. The two sharp peaks at 2916 cm−1 and 1448 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of CH (Ming et al., 2019). As expected, there was an abundance of hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups distributed on the surface of the CDs, which would be beneficial for responding to and sequestering heavy metal ions.

Since they are expected to possess excellent optical properties, Fe3O4/CDs have been independently studied for their absorption and fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2 ). In Fig. 2a, the UV/vis spectrum of Fe3O4/CDs exhibit a typical peak at 345 nm, which can be attributed to n-π* transition (Wang et al., 2018). The maximum emission is centered at 435 nm with excitation at 360 nm. The corresponding picture, showed in the inset of Fig. 2A, shows Fe3O4/CDs solution glowing an intense blue emission under the UV illumination. Fluorescence spectra of Fe3O4/CDs excitated at a range of 280 ~ 420 nm were estimated to elucidate optimum conditions. Compared with the intensity of the emission at different excitation, 360 nm was considered as the optimal wavelength (Fig. 2B). The time-resolved fluorescence decay of Fe3O4/CDs was estimated by single and double-exponential function, manifesting the average lifetimes of 13.7 ns (Fig. 2C). The quantum yield of Fe3O4/CDs was calculated to be 58%, determined by integrating emission intensity and absorbance (supporting information).

Excellent fluorescence stability endows the fluorescence sensors capability and ability. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4/CDs with different store times was tested and displayed in the Fig. S5A. A negligible change in the fluorescence emission can be seen, manifesting excellent stability versus time, which indicated the Fe3O4/CDs can keep long-term storage. Also, the effect of ionic strength of the Fe3O4/CDs was tested by adding NaCl solution (Fig. S5B). When the concentration of the NaCl solution reached 1.0 M, no obvious change of the fluorescence intensity occurred, showing the good tolerance to ionic strength of the Fe3O4/CDs. The pH resistance of the Fe3O4/CDs was further studied and shown in the Fig. S6. The fluorescence emission at different pH was investigated to evaluate the effect of pH on the Fe3O4/CDs. As portrayed in Fig. S6, the fluorescent intensity of Fe3O4/CDs only slightly changed with different pH. It was also can be observed with the naked eye that the fluorescence brightness of the probes at different pH did not change significantly (shown in Fig. S7A), which indicated the good pH resistance of Fe3O4/CDs.

The magnetic behavior of Fe3O4/CDs was assessed by vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) at room temperature (Fig. 3 ). As expected, the Fe3O4/CDs responded well to magnetic fields with the magnetization saturation value of 66. 54 emu·g−1. A hysteresis loop with a coercive field of about 42.75 Oe could be obtained (Fig. 3 inset-up), which indicates that Fe3O4/CDs can be quickly separated by a permanent magnet. As Fig. 3 (inset-down) proved, the Fe3O4/CDs can be condensed within 30 s by an external magnet, which provided the possibility of removal of heavy ions from the water (Tang, Ren, & Chai, 2020). In order to prove the property of magnetic recovery of Fe3O4/CDs, the fluorescence of Fe3O4/CDs before and after exposing to external magnetic field was evaluated. As depicted in the Fig. S8, the same concentration Fe3O4/CDs exhibited different brightness of blue emission; left is normal, and right is with a permanent magnet, which indicated that the fluorescent Fe3O4/CDs possessed excellent magnetic performance, and were easy to recycle. Notably, the CDs were proved to be integrated with Fe3O4 particles, and can be separated by magnetism with Fe3O4 particles, resulting in the reduction of fluorescence intensity.

Based on the above discussion, Fe3O4/CDs with good optical properties can be considered as an excellent fluorescent probe and used to specifically recognize Hg2+. The fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4/CDs mixed with different metal ions (Zn2+, Mn2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe3+ and As3+, 100 μM) solutions were tested (Fig. 4 ). The results showed the individual presence of related ions had lessobvious fluorescence quenching compared to the blank sample, except for Hg2+. However, the Hg2+ sample caused significant reduction of the fluorescence intensity of the Fe3O4/CDs. As shown inset in Fig. 4, other samples showed intense bright blue fluorescence under the UV illumination, and only Hg2+ rendered the blue emission decayed, which was in perfectaccord with the fluorescence spectra results. Therefore, Fe3O4/CDs can be used as a selective fluorescence sensor to specifically recognize Hg2+. The high selectivity of Fe3O4/CDs to Hg(II) can be attributed to the multiple active sites including carboxylate, hydroxyl groups and abundant amino-groups. These led to the interaction between Hg2+ and Fe3O4/CDs, which accelerated the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through an effective electron transfer process (He et al., 2016; Zhou, Lin, Huang, Ren, & Qu, 2012), causing a substantial decrease of the fluorescence of Fe3O4/CDs.

To investigate the feasibility of Fe3O4/CDs monitoring Hg2+ as a fluorescent probe, fluorescence emission of Fe3O4/CDs with different concentrations of Hg2+ (λex = 360 nm) were tested. As can be clearly seen in Fig. 5 A, as the concentration of Hg2+ increases, the fluorescence intensity of the Fe3O4/CDs at 440 nm gradually decreased. When the concentration of Hg2+ reached 300 μM, the FL emission was exhausted completely. It was noteworthy that the fluorescence intensity ratio in the absence and presence of Hg2+ displayed a linear response to the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.003 ~ 0.01 μM. The corresponding linear equation was I/I0 = -8.2873x + 1.0175 (R2 = 0.997) in Fig. 5B (I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of Fe3O4/CDs with and without Hg2+, respectively). Finally, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.30 nM based on 3δ/k (Zhou, et al., 2016). It was observed under UV light that as Hg2+ increased, the fluorescence intensity of the Fe3O4/CDs by degrees attenuated, which was consistent with the fluorescence spectrum results (Fig. 5C). To evaluate the detecting performance of the Fe3O4/CDs, the comparison with reported works were carried out and listed in Table S1. The LOD of this work exhibited certain advantages and was lower than the standard in drinking waterfor the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (10 nM), which indicated the potential for Fe3O4/CDs as fluorescent probes (Yu, Zhang, Ren, Li, Zhu, Chai, et al., 2018).

The recycling of Fe3O4/CDs was an important parameter for the practical application of the analysis system. As depicted in Fig. S9A-I and S9A-II, the fluorescence intensity of Fe3O4/CDs was evidently reduced after exposing to Hg2+. However, the emission intensity was almost fully recovered under the action of saturated EDTA solution (Fig.S9A-III). The fluorescence intensity recovered more than 95% compared with the blank solution. The corresponding phenomenon was also surveyed under the UV lamp. The blue fluorescence of the Fe3O4/CDs solution was turned off after exposing to Hg2+, and the fluorescence can be regained after addition of the saturated EDTA solution (inset of Fig. S9A). It can be seen from Fig. S9B that the fluorescence intensity is enhanced as the EDTA concentration is increased from 0.05 ~ 50 mM. EDTA has a strong chelation effect due to its plentiful –NH2 and –COOH groups, which can attract Hg2+ from the Fe3O4/CDs-Hg2+ system and capture it from its combination with Fe3O4/CDs, resulting in the restored fluorescence of Fe3O4/CDs (Li et al., 2019). These results indicated that the Fe3O4/CDs solution can be recovered as a fluorescent sensor for detecting Hg2+.

To further investigate the effect of Fe3O4/CDs on the removal of Hg2+ in water, ICP tests were performed on samples that were treated with Fe3O4/CDs. The 50 μM Hg2+ was diluted 5000 times with ultrapure water and then mixed with Fe3O4/CDs with the volume ratio of 1:1. Next, a permanent magnet was put on the bottom of the sample for 2 min, and the supernatant was extracted for ICP testing. The final concentration of Hg2+ tested by ICP was 0.005 μM. As can be seen from Table S2, the Hg2+ content in ultrapure water was almost zero (sample I). After adding Hg2+ to ultrapure water, the Hg2+ content reached 0.9838 ppb (≈0.0049 μM) (sample II). Fe3O4/CDs were added and shaken, and after standing for a certain period of time, the Fe3O4/CDs were removed using a magnet. The Hg2+ content in the obtained solution was measured to be 0.4114 ppb (0.002 μM) (sample III). The Hg2+ content in the solution was reduced, which indicated that the Fe3O4/CDs had a certain adsorption effect on the Hg2+. The performance of removal of Hg2+ from water revealed the Fe3O4/CDs offered great potential for detection and recovery of heavy metals from environmental water. To estimate the performance of removal under different conditions, pH, temperature, and kinetic studies were further carried out. The fluorescence emission of Fe3O4/CDs extinguished with 100 μM of Hg2+ at pH 3–12, which indicated the detection was feasible over a large scale of pH (Fig. S7B). The removal of Hg2+ using Fe3O4/CDs can be verified by ICP. The procedure of comparison was performed to recover the Hg2+ sample (14 ppm) at different pH and temperature (Table S3 and Table S4), and tested for Hg2+ after magnetic removal of the Fe3O4/CDs–Hg2+ by a permanent magnet. That the removal can be accomplished at different pH and temperature, manifested the environmental tolerance of Fe3O4/CDs. The kinetic study disclosed that the removal can be fulfilled within two minutes (Fig. S10). In order to clarify respective roles of CDs and Fe3O4 in the process of detection and removal of Hg2+, the CDs and Fe3O4 were synthesized separately by the same procedure, and the comparative experiments were carried out (Supplementary material, Fig. S11, Table S5). The results indicated that the detection performance can be attributed to the function of CDs, and removal of Hg2+ could not be accomplished by Fe3O4 separately; the collection of Hg2+ by CDs played a crucial role in the process of recycling. Therefore, the removal of Hg2+ should be attributed to the synergism of Fe3O4 and CDs, and the Fe3O4/CDs can be considered as an efficient probe for sensing and removal of Hg2+.

From the above research results, it can be concluded that the Fe3O4/CDs exhibited good specific recognition and high sensitivity to Hg2+. Thus, the behavior of the probe for sensing Hg2+ in food and environmental samples was also expanded to lake water, tap water and drinks. According to the reported literature, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency has set a toxic level for Hg2+ at 10 nM. In the samples tested, Hg2+ was not detected (Fig. S12 and Table S6), indicating that their concentrations were lower than the LOD of 0.30 nM. In other words, the content of Hg2+ was so low that it has almost no effect on our measurements. Then the specificity was estimated in Fig. S13 by using spiking method (Yu, Zhang, Ren, Li, Zhu, Chai, et al., 2018). Fig. S13A showed emission of Fe3O4/CDs exposed to 100 μM of other competitive ions (Zn2+, Mn2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Cr6+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe3+ and As3+) and Hg2+ in lake water. The Fe3O4/CDs were significantly quenched after the addition of Hg2+. Blue emission was quenched after meeting Hg2+, which could be observed under an UV lamp (Fig. S13B). The results were consistent with ultrapure water. Although the lake contains a variety of minerals and organic matter, the Fe3O4/CDs were still highly selective for the detection of Hg2+ in actual water samples. Similarly, various concentrations of the lake sample were tested (shown in Fig. S14). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity declined with the increase of Hg2+ concentration (Fig. S14A). The corresponding phenomenon can also be observed from the fluorescent images under the UV lamp (Fig. S14B). The recovery of Hg2+ in Chagan Lake water and tap water was calculated at 96.5 ~ 104.7%, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were found less than 6.0% (Table S7), the recovery in drinks was 96.7 ~ 108.8%, with RSD of 0.83% (Table S8), demonstrating the feasibility of the Fe3O4/CDs sensing system in natural samples. These results manifested that the tracing of Hg2+ in the actual environment by Fe3O4/CDs was practicable.

In general, fluorescence quenching of Fe3O4/CDs resulted from dynamic quenching (electron transfer processes) or static quenching (formation of non-photoluminescent complexes). The fluorescence lifetime spectrum was generally considered to be an effective means of distinguishing between the two mechanisms. Therefore, the lifetime of Fe3O4/CDs was evaluated in detail with and without Hg2+, and the corresponding spectrum was placed in the Fig. S15. The average fluorescence lifetime of Fe3O4/CDs can be calculated to be 13.00 ns (Fig. S15A). After mixing with Hg2+, the fluorescence lifetime of the Fe3O4/CDs was reduced to 10.71 ns (Fig. S15B). The fluorescence lifetime was decreased significantly by Hg2+, indicating a dynamic quenching between Fe3O4/CDs and Hg2+. In addition, the absorption of Hg2+ (Fig. S16) shows a considerable spectral overlap with the excitation of Fe3O4/CDs, which proves the existence of the inner-filter effect (IFE) in the process of quenching of Fe3O4/CDs with Hg2+.

In this work, dual-functional, low-cost magnetic carbon dot nanocomposites Fe3O4/CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. With excellent fluorescence properties and unique magnetic behavior, the Fe3O4/CDs were exploited to be a dual-functional fluorescent probe. In this present sensing system, Hg2+ can efficiently coordinate onto the surface of Fe3O4/CDs by means of electrostatic interactions and remarkably quench the fluorescence signal of Fe3O4/CDs as a result of the fluorescence charge transfer (turn-off). The Fe3O4/CDs exhibited many advantageous features over the current single-function probe including Hg2+ removal, reusability, environmental tolerance and low cost. The results show that Fe3O4/CDs as a fluorescence sensor has a wide application prospect in the detection and removal of Hg2+ in the actual environment.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Heilongjiang Postdoctoral fund (LBH-Q17084), the Fundamental Research Funds for Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (2018-KYYWF-0995), Beihai Municipal Science and Technology Bureau (202082024), the Excellent Scientific Research Team Project of Harbin Normal University (XKYT202001), Innovation Foundation of Graduate Student of Harbin Normal University (HSDSSCX2021-38) and the China Scholarship Council (201908230064).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.130366.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
